heroOverview: A brain arteriovenous malformation (AVM) is a tangle of abnormal blood vessels — the nidus — that directly connects arterial to venous circulation without an intervening capillary bed, generating high-pressure arteriovenous shunting and fragile vessel walls prone to rupture. Brain AVMs are identified in approximately 1.1-1.4 per 100,000 adults per year and carry an annual rupture risk of 2-4%, producing intracerebral hemorrhage with a one-time mortality of 10-30% and a significant disability rate. Seizures are the presenting feature in 20-25% of unruptured AVMs; headaches and focal neurological deficits account for most remaining presentations. Microsurgical resection, stereotactic radiosurgery, endovascular embolization, or a combination of these are the principal interventions — selection is guided by the Spetzler-Martin grading system.

keyStats.prevalence: Incidence approximately 1.1 per 100,000 person-years; prevalence estimated 10-18 per 100,000; roughly 300,000 Americans living with a known brain AVM
keyStats.demographics: Diagnosed equally in men and women; detected most commonly between ages 20-40; HHT-associated AVMs occur across all ages
keyStats.avgAge: Mean age at first hemorrhage approximately 33 years; mean age at diagnosis (unruptured) approximately 31 years
keyStats.globalCases: Approximately 3,000-5,000 AVM ruptures per year in the United States; accounts for 2% of all hemorrhagic strokes

definition: Arteriovenous malformation (ICD-10: Q28.2 for cerebral; D18.09 for vascular malformation) is a congenital vascular anomaly in which arteries connect directly to veins through a disordered cluster of dysplastic channels — the nidus — without a normal capillary network. The nidus receives high-pressure arterial flow; veins become arterialized, dilated, and thin-walled, with markedly increased risk of hemorrhage. Brain AVMs are classified as parenchymal (within brain tissue) or dural/pial (on the surface), with the former carrying higher hemorrhage risk. The Spetzler-Martin grading system (Grade I-V) scores AVMs on nidus size (small <3 cm = 1 point; medium 3-6 cm = 2 points; large >6 cm = 3 points), venous drainage (superficial = 0; deep = 1), and eloquent location (non-eloquent = 0; eloquent = 1). Grade I-II AVMs are favorable for surgery; Grade IV-V have prohibitive surgical morbidity and are managed with radiosurgery or embolization. Hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia (HHT, Osler-Weber-Rendu syndrome), caused by mutations in ENG or ACVRL1, is the main genetic condition associated with multiple cerebral, pulmonary, and hepatic AVMs. Sporadic AVMs arise from somatic mutations in KRAS, BRAF, and GNAQ. Annual hemorrhage risk is approximately 2-4% for unruptured AVMs and increases to 6-18% per year in the first 1-2 years after a prior bleed. Neurosurgery, interventional neuroradiology, and radiation oncology manage AVMs in a multimodal team.

typesClassification.description: Nidus located within brain parenchyma. Classified by Spetzler-Martin grade I-V. Grade I-II AVMs are amenable to low-morbidity microsurgical resection. Accounts for the majority of symptomatic presentations with hemorrhage, seizure, and focal deficits.
Nidus at the cortical surface without deep parenchymal extension. Generally Grade I-II on Spetzler-Martin scoring. Favorable for surgical resection with low morbidity.

Nidus in the brainstem, basal ganglia, thalamus, corpus callosum, or language/motor cortex. High Spetzler-Martin grade (III-V). Surgical morbidity prohibitive; stereotactic radiosurgery is the preferred active treatment.

Hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia (HHT) causes multiple small brain AVMs alongside pulmonary and hepatic AVMs. SMAD4 mutations in HHT type 3 also cause juvenile polyposis. Management is syndromic and involves a multisystem HHT specialist center.

Abnormal connections within the dura mater, distinct from parenchymal AVMs. Presents with pulsatile tinnitus, orbital bruit, or hemorrhage depending on venous drainage pattern. Treated primarily by endovascular embolization.

primarySymptoms: Spontaneous intracerebral hemorrhage presenting as sudden severe headache, focal neurological deficit, loss of consciousness, or seizure — the most feared and common first presentation, occurring in 50% of diagnosed AVMs.
Focal seizures (partial onset) in 20-25% of patients with unruptured cortical AVMs, arising from irritative effects of the AVM or prior small hemorrhages on adjacent cortex.
Chronic headache in 15-20% of AVM patients, often unilateral and localized over the AVM site — mimicking migraine but distinguished by location consistency and imaging findings.

Progressive focal neurological deficit — weakness, speech difficulty, visual field loss, or sensory change — in 5-10% of patients, caused by vascular steal from the AVM diverting blood from adjacent normal brain.
Pulsatile tinnitus or audible bruit in the head or behind an eye in patients with large AVMs or dural arteriovenous fistulas near the transverse or sigmoid sinus.
Transient ischemic attack-like episodes from hemodynamic steal or microemboli from the AVM into adjacent arterial territories.
Papilledema and visual symptoms from raised intracranial pressure caused by large AVMs obstructing venous outflow or causing obstructive hydrocephalus.
Sudden thunderclap headache from AVM rupture — distinguishable from primary headache by immediate maximum intensity and associated neurological symptoms.
earlyWarningSigns: New-onset focal seizures in a young adult, particularly without fever or metabolic cause, warrant urgent gadolinium MRI to exclude an AVM.
Recurrent unilateral headache with a consistent location in a young person, where migraine is the initial diagnosis but brain imaging has not been performed.
Transient focal neurological symptoms (arm weakness, speech stumble, visual flicker) in a young adult without cardiovascular risk factors.
First-degree relative with HHT — warrants MRI brain screening for cerebral AVMs given 5-10% intracranial AVM prevalence in HHT families.
emergencySigns: Sudden severe headache with maximum intensity at onset, neck stiffness, vomiting, or loss of consciousness — requires emergency CT head without contrast to exclude intracranial hemorrhage from AVM rupture.
Acute focal neurological deficit (facial droop, arm weakness, aphasia, visual loss) with sudden onset — AVM hemorrhage must be excluded before ischemic stroke treatment is initiated.
Seizure followed by a persistent focal deficit (Todd's paralysis) in a previously well young adult — brain AVM is a cause until excluded by imaging.
Pupil asymmetry, declining consciousness, or Cushing's triad (bradycardia, hypertension, irregular breathing) indicating impending cerebral herniation from large AVM hematoma.

causes.cause: Somatic KRAS, BRAF, or MAP2K1 mutations in vascular endothelial cells
causes.description: Recent genomic studies demonstrate that the majority of sporadic brain AVMs harbor somatic gain-of-function mutations in RAS-MAPK pathway genes — particularly KRAS G12 variants — in vascular endothelial cells of the nidus. These mutations activate endothelial VEGF signaling and angiogenesis, leading to the formation of the dysplastic arteriovenous shunting channels during embryogenesis or early postnatal vascular remodeling.

Hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia types 1 and 2 are caused by autosomal dominant loss-of-function mutations in ENG (endoglin, chromosome 9) or ACVRL1 (ALK1, chromosome 12), both of which encode TGF-beta pathway co-receptors. Loss of signaling dysregulates endothelial cell identity and vessel specification, permitting arteriovenous shunt formation in multiple organs.

During fetal brain development, primitive arteriovenous channels that normally regress and form capillary beds may persist due to local signaling errors. AVMs are therefore considered predominantly congenital lesions, present at birth but often undetected until hemorrhage or seizure in adulthood.

causes.cause: De novo AVM formation after brain injury or radiation
causes.description: A small number of case series describe de novo AVM formation years after brain radiation or injury, particularly in children. The mechanism involves radiation-induced vascular endothelial injury and dysregulated angiogenesis. These lesions represent a small minority of all AVMs.

riskFactors.description: The annualized rupture risk after a first hemorrhage rises to 6-18% in the first 1-2 years before returning to baseline levels of 2-4%. Prior hemorrhage is the single strongest predictor of re-bleeding.

AVMs draining exclusively into deep veins (internal cerebral vein, Vein of Galen, basal vein of Rosenthal) carry a higher annual hemorrhage risk than superficially draining AVMs. Deep drainage is captured in the Spetzler-Martin scoring system.

Basal ganglia, thalamic, and posterior fossa (brainstem, cerebellar) AVMs have higher hemorrhage rates (approximately 10% per year) compared with supratentorial superficial AVMs (~2.5% per year) based on large natural history cohort data.

Counterintuitively, smaller AVMs have higher hemorrhage rates per unit size because they have higher feeding artery pressures and more concentrated flow. Large AVMs may bleed less per year but cause more devastating hemorrhage when they do.

HHT (ENG or ACVRL1 mutations) increases the probability of multiple cerebral AVMs. HHT-related brain AVMs are often smaller, multiple, and detected earlier through systematic screening in affected families.
Flow-related aneurysms occur on AVM feeding arteries in 7-14% of patients and significantly increase rupture risk. Management of the aneurysm is often the first priority in treatment planning.

affectedDemographics: Brain AVMs affect men and women approximately equally; slightly higher incidence in men in some series.
Presentation most commonly between ages 20 and 40; hemorrhage is the most frequent presentation at all ages.
HHT-associated cerebral AVMs are found in approximately 5-10% of HHT patients and affect all ages; screening in HHT families should begin in childhood.
No clear ethnic predisposition identified for sporadic AVMs; HHT is overrepresented in some geographic populations (e.g., Jura region of France, Afrikaner population).
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of brain AVM follows a structured neuroradiological workup. CT head without contrast is the fastest initial test when hemorrhage is suspected and identifies hyperdense collections. MRI with gadolinium provides superior anatomical detail, showing the nidus, feeding arteries, draining veins, and adjacent parenchymal injury or hemosiderin from prior microhemorrhage. Magnetic resonance angiography (MRA) provides non-invasive vascular mapping for initial treatment planning. Digital subtraction angiography (DSA) remains the gold standard for full characterization of AVM anatomy — identifying all feeding arteries, the nidus architecture, venous drainage pattern, associated aneurysms, and hemodynamic flow — and is performed in preparation for intervention. BOLD functional MRI and tractography are used to map eloquent cortex and white matter tracts adjacent to the AVM nidus, informing surgical risk assessment. Spetzler-Martin grading is calculated from the DSA and MRI: nidus size, eloquence of adjacent brain, and venous drainage pattern. For HHT patients, screening with MRI of the entire brain identifies additional asymptomatic AVMs. Follow-up imaging after treatment by radiosurgery requires annual MRI for 3-5 years until obliteration is confirmed by DSA, which remains the definitive test for cure.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line test for suspected AVM hemorrhage; identifies acute intracranial blood, hematoma volume, and hydrocephalus
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 5-minute scan with no contrast injection. Available 24 hours a day in emergency settings. Hyperdense blood is immediately visible. Sensitivity for AVM nidus is low — follow-up MRI is required after the acute phase.

diagnosticTests.test: MRI brain with and without gadolinium
diagnosticTests.purpose: Defines nidus location, size, relationship to eloquent cortex, and evidence of prior microhemorrhage (hemosiderin on gradient echo/SWI sequences)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-45 minutes in MRI scanner; gadolinium injected via arm cannula. Dedicated AVM protocol includes gradient echo, time-of-flight MRA, and contrast-enhanced T1. Results interpreted by a neuroradiologist with AVM experience.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard test for full hemodynamic and anatomical AVM characterization before intervention; identifies feeding arteries, nidus, draining veins, and flow-related aneurysms
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Invasive procedure under local anaesthesia or sedation; catheter placed via femoral artery to selective renal artery injections. Takes 60-120 minutes. Carries 0.5-1% risk of stroke or access-site complications. Required before surgery or embolization.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Maps language, motor, sensory, and visual cortex relative to the AVM nidus; identifies white matter tracts at risk during surgery
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-60 minutes; patient performs cognitive tasks during scanning (e.g., tapping fingers, listening to words). No contrast needed. Results guide surgical planning and patient counseling on neurological risk.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Rapid non-invasive vascular imaging; used in emergency hemorrhage workup to identify the AVM nidus when DSA is not immediately available
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 5-10 minutes; iodinated contrast injected via arm cannula. Rapid image reconstruction identifies feeding arteries and nidus in emergency settings. Not a substitute for DSA in treatment planning.

treatmentOverview: AVM management follows a risk-benefit framework comparing the natural history rupture risk against procedural risks of intervention. For unruptured AVMs, the ARUBA trial (2014) — a landmark randomized trial comparing intervention to observation in unruptured brain AVMs — reported that medical management alone resulted in fewer strokes and deaths than interventional therapy at 33 months follow-up. Long-term ARUBA follow-up (7.5 years) confirmed sustained benefit for medical management. This challenged the previous assumption that all AVMs should be treated, and current ACC/AHA guidelines recommend against routine intervention for unruptured AVMs based on ARUBA data. Active treatment — surgery, embolization, radiosurgery, or combinations — is favored for patients with prior hemorrhage, rapidly progressive neurological deficit, refractory seizures, or young patients with Grade I-II AVMs where intervention cure rates are high and procedural morbidity is low. Microsurgical resection provides immediate anatomical cure for accessible low-grade AVMs; outcomes depend critically on Spetzler-Martin grade. Stereotactic radiosurgery (Gamma Knife, CyberKnife) obliterates small-to-medium AVMs over 2-3 years; the latency period during which hemorrhage risk persists before obliteration is complete is the main drawback. Endovascular embolization using liquid embolic agents (Onyx, NBCA) rarely achieves complete AVM obliteration alone (5-10% cure rate) but reduces nidus volume before surgery or radiosurgery as an adjunct. For deeply located high-grade AVMs not amenable to surgery, radiosurgery or combined embolization plus radiosurgery is the standard active treatment option.

medicalTreatments.name: Observation with surveillance MRI (for unruptured AVMs)
medicalTreatments.description: Active management with annual or biennial MRI without intervention, based on ARUBA trial evidence showing superior outcomes vs intervention for unruptured AVMs at 7.5-year follow-up. Antiepileptic drugs are given if seizures are present. Hypertension is controlled to avoid blood pressure spikes.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ARUBA trial: medical management alone associated with 75% relative risk reduction in stroke or death compared with interventional management at 33 months in unruptured AVMs

medicalTreatments.name: Antiepileptic therapy (levetiracetam 500-1500 mg twice daily or valproate 500-1000 mg twice daily)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line treatment for AVM-related seizures. Seizure control is achieved in 60-70% of patients with single-agent therapy. Levetiracetam is preferred due to tolerability and lack of enzyme induction affecting subsequent treatment planning.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Seizure freedom in approximately 60-70% with appropriate antiepileptic drug monotherapy; residual seizures may persist until AVM is obliterated

medicalTreatments.name: Blood pressure control (target <130/80 mmHg)
medicalTreatments.description: Hypertension significantly worsens hemorrhage risk from AVMs. All patients with known AVMs should achieve and maintain target blood pressure. ACE inhibitors, ARBs, and calcium channel blockers are preferred; avoid agents causing acute blood pressure swings.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Observational data associate BP control with reduced hemorrhage risk; no specific randomized trial in AVM patients

surgicalOptions.name: Microsurgical AVM resection
surgicalOptions.description: Open craniotomy with intraoperative neurophysiology monitoring to identify and divide feeding arteries before removal of the nidus. Provides immediate and definitive cure when complete obliteration is achieved. Best for Spetzler-Martin Grade I-II AVMs in non-eloquent locations. Intraoperative DSA confirms complete resection.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Grade I: complete obliteration 95-100%, major neurological deficit <2%. Grade II: obliteration 90-95%, deficit 5-10%. Grade III-V: obliteration rates decline and morbidity increases substantially

surgicalOptions.name: Stereotactic radiosurgery (Gamma Knife, CyberKnife)
surgicalOptions.description: Outpatient radiosurgery delivering 18-25 Gy to the AVM nidus. Causes progressive endothelial proliferation and thrombosis within the nidus over 2-3 years. Preferred for small-to-medium AVMs (under 3 cm nidus) in eloquent or deep locations unsuitable for surgery.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Obliteration at 2-3 years in 75-85% of AVMs under 3 cm; hemorrhage risk during latency period persists at approximately 1-2% per year until obliteration is confirmed on DSA

surgicalOptions.name: Endovascular embolization (Onyx, NBCA liquid embolic)
surgicalOptions.description: Transarterial injection of liquid embolic material into feeding arteries of the AVM nidus via a microcatheter under DSA guidance. Rarely curative alone (complete occlusion in under 10% of cases) but reduces nidus volume by 30-50% as adjunct before surgery or radiosurgery.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Curative embolization alone: 5-10%. Pre-surgical embolization reduces intraoperative blood loss and facilitates complete resection; pre-radiosurgery embolization reduces target volume in large AVMs

surgicalOptions.name: Combined embolization followed by radiosurgery
surgicalOptions.description: Endovascular reduction of nidus volume followed by stereotactic radiosurgery of the residual nidus. Used for medium-to-large AVMs where surgery is too risky and radiosurgery alone is limited by nidus size.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Obliteration in 50-70% of medium AVMs at 3 years with combined approach; outcomes depend on adequacy of embolization and residual nidus volume

whySeeSpecialist: All newly diagnosed brain AVMs should be evaluated by a multidisciplinary neurovascular team including a neurosurgeon, interventional neuroradiologist, and radiation oncologist before any treatment decision. Spetzler-Martin grading and functional MRI interpretation require specialist expertise. The ARUBA trial outcome underscores that the decision not to intervene is as valid as treatment — and requires the same level of specialist review.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a center that performs more than 30 AVM surgeries per year; outcome data demonstrate clear volume-outcome relationships for microsurgical resection. Radiosurgery centers should have a minimum 5-year AVM obliteration follow-up database to quote realistic outcomes. Joint neurovascular conference with neurosurgery, neuroradiology, and radiation oncology is the standard at high-volume centers.
hospitalCriteria: Dedicated cerebrovascular or neurointerventional DSA suite available 24 hours for hemorrhage workup
High-volume neurosurgery center with AVM microsurgery experience and intraoperative neurophysiology monitoring
Gamma Knife or dedicated linear accelerator radiosurgery facility
Neurointensive care unit for post-hemorrhage and post-operative management
Functional MRI and diffusion tractography available for surgical planning
keyFacilities: Academic neurosurgery programs with dedicated AVM team
Gamma Knife-equipped radiosurgery centers with AVM obliteration registry
HHT Centers of Excellence for hereditary multiple AVM management
preventionStrategies: Sporadic brain AVMs are congenital and cannot be prevented. Genetic counseling for HHT families allows early identification and surveillance imaging before hemorrhage occurs.
Control blood pressure rigorously in all known AVM patients; acute hypertension dramatically increases rupture risk.
Avoid anticoagulants and antiplatelet agents in patients with known unruptured AVMs unless there is a compelling concurrent indication.
Screen first-degree relatives of HHT patients with brain MRI from adolescence, as 5-10% will have cerebral AVMs.
lifestyleModifications: Maintain blood pressure below 130/80 mmHg with lifestyle measures and medications; avoid stimulants that cause acute pressure spikes.
During the observation period, carry a concise medical alert describing the AVM diagnosis, size, and location for emergency responders.
Avoid high-intensity physical exertion (heavy lifting, intense aerobic exercise) that causes acute blood pressure elevation until the AVM has been treated and confirmed obliterated.
Avoid anticoagulants (warfarin, direct oral anticoagulants) and high-dose aspirin unless the indication outweighs the hemorrhage risk; discuss with the neurovascular team.
After Gamma Knife, maintain scheduled MRI and DSA follow-up without delay — incomplete obliteration at 3 years warrants retreatment consideration.

recommended: Low-sodium diet (under 2 g/day) to support blood pressure control
Mediterranean-pattern diet rich in fruits, vegetables, olive oil, and fish to reduce cardiovascular risk
Adequate hydration to avoid dehydration-related blood pressure fluctuation
Foods rich in vitamin K consistency if anticoagulation decision is being made — stable dietary vitamin K intake simplifies anticoagulant management if ever required
avoid: Excessive alcohol consumption (more than 2 units/day), which impairs blood pressure control and elevates hemorrhage risk
High stimulant intake (caffeine, energy drinks), which can transiently raise blood pressure and precipitate AVM bleeding
NSAIDs and high-dose aspirin without specialist guidance, as antiplatelet effect may worsen hemorrhage outcomes
Illicit stimulant drugs (cocaine, methamphetamine), which are associated with spontaneous intracranial hemorrhage regardless of AVM status

exerciseGuidelines: Low-to-moderate aerobic exercise (walking, cycling, gentle swimming) is generally safe for patients under observation for small unruptured AVMs after neurovascular team review. Strenuous or high-intensity exercise — valsalva maneuvers, heavy weight-lifting, competitive contact sports — should be avoided due to acute blood pressure surges. After confirmed AVM obliteration (DSA), activity restrictions can be lifted progressively.
dailyManagement: Take antiepileptic medications at consistent times each day; sudden discontinuation risks breakthrough seizures.
Monitor blood pressure at home twice daily; keep a log and share it with the neurovascular team.
Attend all scheduled MRI and DSA follow-up appointments — surveillance for obliteration or re-growth is mandatory.
Wear a medical alert bracelet stating AVM diagnosis, location, and emergency contact for the treating center.
Report new or changed headache, new focal neurological symptoms, or any seizure immediately to the neurovascular team.
prognosis: Prognosis depends heavily on whether the AVM bleeds and what treatment is undertaken. For unruptured AVMs managed by observation, the ARUBA trial demonstrated that medical management alone results in fewer adverse events at 7.5 years than intervention. Annual rupture risk is approximately 2-4%, with each hemorrhage carrying 10-30% mortality and 30-50% permanent disability risk. Successful microsurgical resection of Grade I-II AVMs achieves cure with under 5% morbidity. Gamma Knife achieves obliteration in 75-85% of small AVMs at 3 years, but hemorrhage risk persists during the latency period. For deep eloquent high-grade AVMs, no treatment has demonstrated risk-benefit superiority over observation. Patients who survive the first hemorrhage without major deficit and receive timely treatment have a good functional prognosis in the majority of cases. Long-term neurological stability after complete obliteration is achieved in over 90% of treated Grade I-II AVMs.
recoveryTimeline: After microsurgery, hospital stay is 3-7 days for uncomplicated cases; return to normal activity in 4-8 weeks. After Gamma Knife, no hospitalization is required; AVM obliteration occurs over 2-3 years as confirmed by DSA. After AVM hemorrhage, intensive care management for 1-2 weeks is typical; neurological rehabilitation continues for 3-12 months depending on the hemorrhage volume and location.
complications: Intracerebral hemorrhage — annual risk 2-4% for untreated AVMs, 6-18% in the first 1-2 years after prior bleed; each rupture carries 10-30% mortality and significant disability.
Seizure disorder from cortical irritation by the AVM or hemosiderin from prior microhemorrhage — present in 20-25% of patients; requires ongoing antiepileptic medication.
Progressive neurological deficit from vascular steal — the AVM diverts blood from adjacent normal cortex, causing slowly worsening focal weakness, speech, or visual deficits.
Post-radiosurgery radiation injury (1-5% of Gamma Knife-treated AVMs) — perilesional edema causing new focal deficits 6-18 months after treatment; usually resolves with corticosteroids.
Hydrocephalus from intraventricular hemorrhage or venous hypertension impeding CSF absorption — requires urgent CSF diversion.
Post-operative re-bleeding from residual AVM nidus not confirmed obliterated on intraoperative DSA — underscores the importance of intraoperative angiography during all AVM resections.

supportResources.name: Aneurysm and AVM Foundation
supportResources.description: US patient organization providing AVM educational resources, peer support forums, and neurovascular specialist referrals.

supportResources.name: HHT Foundation International
supportResources.description: Information and clinical center directory for patients with hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia and associated brain or pulmonary AVMs.

supportResources.name: American Association of Neurological Surgeons — AVM information
supportResources.description: Authoritative patient-facing information on brain AVM diagnosis, grading systems, and treatment options from the neurosurgical specialty society.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cerebral Aneurysm
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cerebral aneurysms are saccular outpouchings of arterial walls, not arteriovenous tangles. Aneurysm rupture also causes sudden severe headache but produces subarachnoid hemorrhage rather than parenchymal hematoma. DSA distinguishes the two; treatment strategies differ — aneurysms are clipped or coil-embolized while AVMs require resection or radiosurgery.

confusedWithConditions.name: Brain Tumor (Glioma)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Gliomas are infiltrative neoplasms with abnormal tumor cells; AVMs are vascular anomalies without abnormal cell proliferation. Gliomas show mass effect, heterogeneous enhancement, and surrounding edema on MRI. AVMs show tangle of flow voids on T2-weighted MRI without solid tumor matrix. Biopsy is required to distinguish hemorrhagic glioma from AVM in atypical cases.

confusedWithConditions.name: Epilepsy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Primary epilepsy has no structural lesion on imaging. AVM-related seizures arise from an identifiable lesion on MRI. Any new-onset focal seizure in an adult without a prior epilepsy diagnosis warrants gadolinium MRI to exclude an AVM, tumor, or cavernoma before labeling as idiopathic epilepsy.

relatedConditions.name: Cerebral Aneurysm
relatedConditions.relevance: Flow-related aneurysms on AVM feeding arteries occur in 7-14% of AVMs and increase hemorrhage risk; both conditions are managed at cerebrovascular centers.

relatedConditions.name: Stroke
relatedConditions.relevance: AVM hemorrhage is a cause of hemorrhagic stroke in young adults; ischemic stroke can also occur from emboli or vascular steal.

relatedConditions.name: Epilepsy
relatedConditions.relevance: AVM is a structural cause of symptomatic focal epilepsy; seizures may be the only manifestation of an unruptured AVM.

relatedConditions.name: Hydrocephalus
relatedConditions.relevance: Large AVMs obstructing venous outflow, or post-hemorrhagic ventricular blood, can cause obstructive or communicating hydrocephalus requiring CSF diversion.

faqs.question: What is a brain arteriovenous malformation?
faqs.answer: A brain AVM is a tangle of abnormal blood vessels (the nidus) that directly connects arteries to veins without the normal capillary network. The high-pressure arteriovenous shunting creates fragile vessel walls with an annual rupture risk of 2-4%, causing intracerebral hemorrhage. AVMs affect approximately 1 per 100,000 people per year.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of a brain AVM?
faqs.answer: The most common presentation is intracerebral hemorrhage causing sudden severe headache, focal weakness, or loss of consciousness (50% of cases). Focal seizures occur in 20-25% of unruptured AVMs. Other symptoms include persistent headache, progressive neurological deficits from vascular steal, and pulsatile tinnitus in large lesions.

faqs.question: How dangerous is an AVM?
faqs.answer: The annual rupture risk is 2-4% for untreated AVMs and rises to 6-18% per year in the first two years after a prior bleed. Each hemorrhage carries a 10-30% mortality risk and 30-50% risk of permanent disability. For unruptured AVMs, the ARUBA trial showed that observation may be safer than intervention for many patients.

faqs.question: How is a brain AVM diagnosed?
faqs.answer: MRI with gadolinium shows the nidus, feeding arteries, and draining veins. CT head is the first test when hemorrhage is suspected. Digital subtraction angiography (DSA) is the gold standard before intervention, fully characterizing the arterial supply, nidus anatomy, and venous drainage pattern.

faqs.question: Should all brain AVMs be treated?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. The ARUBA trial showed that for unruptured AVMs, observation alone resulted in fewer strokes and deaths than intervention at 7.5 years. Active treatment is favored for patients with prior hemorrhage, low-grade (Spetzler-Martin Grade I-II) lesions where intervention carries low morbidity, refractory seizures, or progressive neurological deficit.

faqs.question: What is the Spetzler-Martin grading system for AVMs?
faqs.answer: The Spetzler-Martin system grades AVMs from I to V based on nidus size (small = 1, medium = 2, large = 3), venous drainage (superficial = 0, deep = 1), and eloquence of adjacent brain (non-eloquent = 0, eloquent = 1). Grade I-II AVMs have low surgical risk. Grade IV-V AVMs have high morbidity rates with surgery.

faqs.question: What is Gamma Knife treatment for AVM?
faqs.answer: Gamma Knife stereotactic radiosurgery delivers a precisely focused high dose of radiation to the AVM nidus in a single outpatient session. Over 2-3 years, the irradiated vessels undergo thrombosis and the nidus obliterates. Obliteration is confirmed by DSA. Success rates are 75-85% for AVMs under 3 cm at 3 years.

faqs.question: Can AVM surgery cause permanent disability?
faqs.answer: Risk depends on the Spetzler-Martin grade. For Grade I AVMs, major neurological deficit occurs in under 2% of cases in experienced centers. For Grade III-IV AVMs, the morbidity rate rises significantly. Intraoperative neurophysiology monitoring, functional MRI planning, and intraoperative DSA verification of complete resection minimize risk.

faqs.question: Can you live a normal life with an AVM?
faqs.answer: Many patients with small unruptured AVMs live normally for decades under observation. The main threats to normal life are hemorrhage and AVM-related seizures. After complete obliteration by surgery or Gamma Knife, most patients with no major prior hemorrhage resume full activity. Annual hemorrhage risk without treatment is 2-4% per year.

faqs.question: Are brain AVMs hereditary?
faqs.answer: Most sporadic brain AVMs are not directly inherited; they arise from somatic mutations in KRAS or related genes. Hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia (HHT), caused by ENG or ACVRL1 gene mutations, causes familial multiple AVMs. First-degree relatives of HHT patients have a 50% inheritance risk and should be screened with brain MRI.

faqs.question: What is embolization for AVM?
faqs.answer: Embolization delivers liquid embolic material (Onyx or NBCA) via a microcatheter into the AVM's feeding arteries to reduce blood flow. It achieves complete cure in only 5-10% of cases when used alone. Most commonly, embolization is used before surgery (to reduce blood loss) or before radiosurgery (to reduce nidus volume).

faqs.question: How long does it take for Gamma Knife to obliterate an AVM?
faqs.answer: Gamma Knife-induced AVM obliteration occurs gradually over 2-3 years after treatment. During this latency period, the annual hemorrhage risk persists at approximately 1-2% per year. Final obliteration is confirmed by digital subtraction angiography (DSA) rather than MRI. About 75-85% of small AVMs are obliterated at 3 years.

faqs.question: Does AVM cause seizures permanently?
faqs.answer: AVM-related seizures may continue until the AVM is obliterated. After complete cure, 50-70% of patients become seizure-free or have substantially improved control. Residual seizures can persist in some patients due to hemosiderin deposits or cortical scarring from prior hemorrhage. Antiepileptic drugs are continued until seizure freedom is established post-treatment.

faqs.question: What happens if an AVM bleeds?
faqs.answer: AVM hemorrhage causes intracerebral bleeding with 10-30% mortality. Survivors face 30-50% rates of permanent neurological deficit depending on hemorrhage location and volume. Emergency CT identifies the bleed; ICU management controls intracranial pressure. Definitive AVM treatment is typically deferred 4-8 weeks to allow hematoma resorption before intervention.

faqs.question: Can AVM recur after surgery?
faqs.answer: Complete microsurgical resection confirmed on intraoperative DSA has under 1-2% recurrence rate in adults. In children, de novo AVM development or residual nidus regrowth occurs in 3-5% of cases; lifelong surveillance imaging is recommended for pediatric patients. Incomplete resection identified postoperatively requires retreatment with radiosurgery or repeat surgery.

faqs.question: How often should an untreated AVM be monitored?
faqs.answer: For unruptured AVMs under observation, annual gadolinium-enhanced MRI is standard. If stable over 5 years, monitoring frequency may decrease to every 2-3 years per institutional protocol. Any change in symptoms — new headache pattern, first seizure, focal neurological change — warrants immediate MRI and neurovascular team review.

symptomKeywords: progressive weakness no stroke risk factors
metaDescription: Brain AVM: abnormal artery-vein tangle with 2-4% annual rupture risk. Hemorrhage, seizure, diagnosis by MRI and DSA, and surgery vs radiosurgery reviewed here.
